title: Souvlaki — pork
subtitle: Grilled pork skewers marinated in lemon, oregano, and olive oil
excerpt: Pork shoulder cubed and marinated in lemon juice, dried oregano, garlic, and olive oil, then grilled on skewers over high heat until charred at the edges and juicy at the centre. Served in pitta with tomato, red onion, and a spoonful of yoghurt.
sourceNotes: Souvlaki (σουβλάκι) is the defining fast food of Greece, documented from ancient Greek sources. The word derives from souvla (skewer). Pork is the most common meat in mainland Greek souvlaki, while lamb and chicken are also widespread. The marinade of lemon, oregano, and olive oil is universal across Greek grilling traditions.

recipe.batchNotes: Marinate large batches and grill in rounds; the skewers can be prepped 24 hours ahead.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The pork can marinate for up to 24 hours in the fridge; the marinade tenderises as well as flavours.

content.text: Souvlaki is not complicated: cube the meat, marinate it, grill it hot. The results depend almost entirely on the quality of the pork and the quality of the heat. Pork shoulder, with its marbling of fat, stays moist on the grill where leaner cuts (loin, tenderloin) dry out before they char. Cut the cubes generously (3 to 4 cm) so the outside can char while the centre stays pink and juicy.
Dried oregano is the correct choice here, not fresh: it has a concentration and intensity that fresh oregano does not. Marinate for at least 2 hours; 24 hours is better.

Combine the {{lemon}} juice, {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}}, {{garlic}}, {{oregano-dried}}, salt, and black pepper in a bowl. Add the {{pork-shoulder}} cubes and toss to coat. Cover and refrigerate for at least 2 hours, or overnight. If using wooden skewers, soak them in water for 30 minutes before threading.

Thread the pork onto skewers, 4 to 5 cubes per skewer. Heat a grill, griddle pan, or barbecue to its highest temperature. Grill the skewers for 10 to 12 minutes, turning every 3 minutes, until charred at the edges and cooked through. Rest for 5 minutes.

Warm the {{pitta-bread}} directly on the grill or in a dry pan. Spread with {{greek-yoghurt}}, add the pork from the skewer, and top with {{tomato}} slices and {{red-onion}}. Squeeze lemon over and eat immediately.

items.cause: Loin or tenderloin used instead of shoulder, or overcooked
items.fix: Use shoulder only for skewers; it has enough fat to stay moist; an internal temperature of 70°C is sufficient — do not overshoot

items.cause: Heat not high enough, or pan overcrowded
items.fix: The grill or griddle must be ripping hot; grill in batches if needed so each skewer has direct contact with the heat

items.symptom: Marinade has made the pork mushy
items.cause: Marinated for too long in too much lemon
items.fix: 24 hours is the maximum; more acid denatures the surface proteins and creates a soft, slightly grey exterior

items.symptom: Pork is charred outside but raw inside
items.cause: Cubes too large, heat too high
items.fix: Keep cubes to 3–4 cm; reduce the heat slightly after the initial char and allow more time on the grill

content.text: Souvlaki is the street food of Greece in the same way that a baguette jambon-beurre is the street food of France: ubiquitous, inexpensive, deeply regional in origin, and now eaten everywhere. Souvladzidika (souvlaki shops) operate from early afternoon until the small hours in most Greek towns, wrapping the skewered meat into warm pitta with tomato, onion, and tzatziki or plain yoghurt. The meat varies by region: Athens tends to pork, Thessaloniki uses both pork and chicken, and Crete has its own skewered lamb traditions. Outside of Greece, the name often defaults to the pitta-wrapped version, which is more properly called pitta souvlaki.
